message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 3/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
DESIGNING AND TRAINING A VOICE / TTS MODEL TO BE MORE COMPREHENSIBLE AT HIGH-SPEED LISTENING. Deliver BOTH the underlying science AND concrete recommendations for a local open-source TTS podcast pipeline (currently uses Kokoro TTS). Cover these 6 angles: (1) ACOUSTIC/PROSODIC PROPERTIES that survive time-compression / make a voice intelligible when sped up — "clear speech"/hyperarticulation, vowel-space expansion, consonant emphasis/sharper consonant-vowel transitions, modulation-spectrum shape, F0/pitch characteristics, and pause/rhythm structure. (2) FORMANT SYNTHESIS vs NEURAL TTS at extreme speed — why blind screen-reader power users overwhelmingly prefer old formant synthesizers (Eloquence, DECtalk, eSpeak-NG) over natural neural voices at 18-22 syllables/second; consistency, crispness, latency, predictability; quantify the naturalness-vs-intelligibility trade-off. (3) RATE-AWARE / CONTROLLABLE TTS GENERATION — conditioning or fine-tuning a neural TTS to NATIVELY generate fast-but-clear speech (speaking-rate control, prosody control, training on clear-speech/Lombard-speech corpora) vs. post-hoc time-scale modification; CRITICAL: how much MEASURABLE comprehensibility gain does native fast generation or fine-tuning actually buy over simply time-compressing a normal voice? (the user will only invest in fine-tuning/training if the gain is significant). (4) DSP ENHANCEMENT for intelligibility at speed — formant/clarity enhancement, spectral shaping + dynamic-range compression, consonant boosting, content-aware PER-PHONEME variable-rate compression (compress steady-state vowels more, preserve consonant transitions), and modulation-envelope enhancement; these apply to ANY engine's output. (5) CONCRETE ENGINES/VOICES & CONFIG KNOBS — head-to-head comparison of which TTS engines/voices are most intelligible at high speed (Kokoro, Piper, eSpeak-NG/Eloquence, RHVoice, Azure/Google neural, ElevenLabs) and the tunable settings (speaking rate, pitch, pause length) that improve speed-comprehensibility. (6) HOW TO MEASURE "comprehensibility at speed" — objective intelligibility metrics (STOI, STMI/spectro-temporal modulation index, modulation-spectrum analysis) and practical subjective A/B / MUSHRA listening-test protocols, so one can decide whether a fine-tune cleared a "significant gain" bar before committing. CONSTRAINTS/CONTEXT: pipeline runs LOCALLY/on-device (open-source Kokoro), so favor approaches compatible with local inference. Output should serve TWO use cases: a normal natural-sounding default voice for general students, AND an optional aggressively speed-optimized track for trained power listeners (robotic is acceptable for that track if measurably clearer). For EACH effort tier (pick/configure a voice < DSP post-processing < fine-tune an open model < train from scratch), state the expected comprehensibility gain, the cost/effort, and whether it is worth it. Prefer peer-reviewed and authoritative sources; include practitioner/community knowledge from the blind/screen-reader speed-listening community where relevant.

## Claim under review
"The processing advantage of linear time-compression over natural fast speech is quantified: in a phoneme-detection task, mean detection time was 572 ms for linear compression vs. 624 ms for natural fast speech — a statistically significant 52 ms advantage (copy-fast-speech-timing was intermediate at 600 ms)."

**Source:** https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092 (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "detection times are fastest in the linear compression condition (572 ms), followed by the copy-fast-speech-timing (600 ms), and the natural fast speech condition (624 ms) ... Only the 52 ms advantage of linear compression over natural fast speech is significant."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

The search results provide relevant information about phoneme detection, linear time compression, and comparisons between natural fast speech and artificially compressed speech. Here are the key findings:

Linear compression of normal speech to a rate of 8.5 syllables per second was associated with significantly faster reaction times in a phoneme detection task compared to naturally fast speech, according to research by Janse (2004).

More broadly, unprocessed fast speech was less intelligible than linearly compressed normal speech, which suggests that the artificial compression method maintains better intelligibility for phoneme detection tasks.

Additionally, both artificial and natural acceleration of speech rate reduce the acoustic signal, but these changes operate non-linearly when naturally speaking at a fast rate, partly due to articulatory restrictions, whereas linear compression applies uniform temporal reduction across the entire utterance.

However, I should note that while the search results discuss phoneme detection tasks with comparisons between linear compression and natural fast speech, the specific time values you mentioned (572 ms and 624 ms) do not appear in these particular search results. If you're looking for a specific study or publication that reports those exact measurements, you may need to search with additional terms or check specialized speech communication databases directly.

Processing of fast rate speech in children with specific language impairment | PLOS One","url":"https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0191808"},{"title":"Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech | Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x"}]

I found relevant search results for your query. Here's what I found:

## Janse (2004) Study Summary

The study by Janse from 2004 was titled "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech" and was published in Speech Communication, volume 42, pages 155-173.

### Key Findings

Janse (2004) reported that normal speech that was linearly compressed to a rate of 8.5 syll/s was associated with significantly faster reaction times in a phoneme detection task compared to naturally fast speech.

### Experimental Design

The stimuli were multisyllabic nouns with a word-initial plosive consonant embedded in sentence fragments, in which the target word-initial plosive did not occur elsewhere in the sentence. The listeners' task was to press a button when they detected a specified word-initial phoneme. The fast-to-normal ratio of the fast-rate speech (both natural and time compressed) was 0.72, which represents a modest increment in speech rate.

This influential study compared how listeners process artificially time-compressed speech versus naturally produced fast speech in phoneme monitoring tasks.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Processing of fast rate speech in children with specific language impairment | PLOS One","url":"https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0191808"}]

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about Janse's 2004 study on word perception in fast speech:

Janse (2004) reported that normal speech that was linearly compressed to a rate of 8.5 syll/s was associated with significantly faster reaction times in a phoneme detection task compared to naturally fast speech.

The study compared different compression methods and found that results in a phoneme detection task showed a processing advantage for linearly time-compressed speech over both non-linearly time-compressed and natural fast speech.

Additionally, even when naturally produced fast speech is perfectly intelligible, its less careful articulation, combined with the changed timing pattern, slows down processing, relative to linearly time-compressed speech. Furthermore, listeners preferred artificially time-compressed speech over naturally produced fast speech. These results suggest that linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech.

The study was published in Speech Communication, volume 42, pages 155-173 (2004).

content.content:
# Janse 2004 Paper: PDF Link and Data

**PDF Link:** Yes, there is a downloadable PDF available. The page provides a "[PDF](https://pure.mpg.de/rest/items/item_66910_4/component/file_66911/content)" link.

**Abstract:** The page contains the study's abstract, which examines how listeners process artificially time-compressed speech versus naturally produced fast speech. Key finding: "linearly time-compressed speech has both a temporal and a segmental advantage over natural fast speech."

**Specific Values:** The page abstract does not contain the specific phoneme detection time values (572, 600, 624 ms) or the "52 ms" significant difference you mentioned. These numerical details are not present in the webpage content provided—they would likely appear in the full PDF paper itself, not on this publication record page.

Processing of fast rate speech in children with specific language impairment | PLOS One","url":"https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0191808"},{"title":"Natural fast speech is perceived as faster than linearly time-compressed speech | Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics | Springer Nature Link","url":"https://link.springer.com/article/10.3758/s13414-016-1067-x"},{"title":"ISCA Archive - Time-compressing natural and synthetic speech","url":"https://www.isca-archive.org/icslp_2002/janse02_icslp.html"},{"title":"[PDF] Production and perception of fast speech | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Production-and-perception-of-fast-speech-Janse/f5d065f2a981a0b0ea2bee72da9e30bbfd47d48e"},{"title":"(PDF) Production and perception of fast speech","url":"https://www.academia.edu/29136550/Production_and_perception_of_fast_speech"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"[PDF] Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Word-perception-in-fast-speech:-artificially-vs.-Janse/e6d93d9bd9bbda8268e21a425250e660cdecfdf2"}]

I found the information you were looking for. Mean detection times (in milliseconds) and miss rates for three fast conditions were: Linear time-compression 572 ms (3% miss rate), Copy-fast-speech-timing 600 ms (3% miss rate), and Natural fast speech 624 ms (3% miss rate).

This data comes from Janse's research on word perception comparing different types of accelerated speech. Janse compared strict linear compression (where all syllables are compressed equally) and non-linear selective compression (which follows the exact temporal pattern of naturally produced fast speech), with results in a phoneme detection task showing a processing advantage for linearly time-compressed speech over both non-linearly time-compressed and natural fast speech.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

of Stress position was far from signiﬁcant
(F1 ð1; 29Þ ¼ 1:0, n.s.; F2 ð1; 82Þ < 1, n.s.); and so
was the interaction between Condition and Stress
position (F1 ð2; 28Þ < 1, n.s.; F2 ð2; 81Þ < 1, n.s.).
Univariate analyses of variance allow the possibility of doing post hoc tests. Condition was
taken as ﬁxed factor, and either subjects or items
as random factors. Missing observations were replaced by either the subject or the item mean in
that condition, depending on the analysis. The
eﬀect of Condition on the inverse detection times
was signiﬁcant in both analyses (F1 ð2; 28Þ ¼ 5:5,
p ¼ 0:007; F2 ð2; 82Þ ¼ 3:1, p ¼ 0:048). Separate
post hoc tests (Scheﬀe) showed which fast speech
conditions diﬀered signiﬁcantly from one another.
The signiﬁcance values of the post hoc analyses are
shown in Table 5.
Only the linear time-compression condition and
the natural fast condition diﬀered signiﬁcantly
from each other: there was no signiﬁcant diﬀerence
between linear compression and the copy-fastspeech-timing condition, and the diﬀerence between the copy-fast-speech-timing condition and

content.text:
I now have the complete primary-source text. Every element of the claim is verified verbatim against Janse (2004), Speech Communication 42, 155-173, the exact source cited.

Verification against the claim:
- "mean detection time was 572 ms for linear compression vs. 624 ms for natural fast speech" — Table 4 and text confirm 572 ms (SE 9) and 624 ms (SE 14). Confirmed.
- "copy-fast-speech-timing was intermediate at 600 ms" — Confirmed (SE 13).
- "a statistically significant 52 ms advantage" — Text states verbatim: "Only the 52 ms advantage of linear compression over natural fast speech is significant." Confirmed.
- The post-hoc detail: "Only the linear time-compression condition and the natural fast condition differed significantly from each other" — fully consistent with the claim. The 52 ms is 624−572 = 52. Arithmetic checks out.

Checklist review:
1. Supported by quote? Yes — the supporting quote matches the PDF verbatim, and the claim accurately paraphrases it. No overreach.
2. Contradicting evidence? None. The general finding (linear compression > natural fast for processing) is independently corroborated by multiple later sources (Janse 2016, follow-up studies). One nuance: significance was on inverse-RT (1/RT) data, not raw means — but the claim's wording ("statistically significant 52 ms advantage") faithfully mirrors the paper's own summary sentence, which itself attaches "significant" to the 52 ms raw-mean advantage. Not a misrepresentation.
3. Source quality? Peer-reviewed primary source (Speech Communication, Elsevier), exactly the cited DOI/PII. Strength matches.
4. Outdated? It's from 2004, but it is a foundational psycholinguistic finding cited to support a specific historical experimental result, not a fast-moving-field benchmark. Appropriate use.
5. Marketing/cherry-picked? No — academic study, full statistical reporting including non-significant comparisons.

The claim is fully supported and not refuted.

input.evidence: VERIFIED against the cited primary source verbatim. Retrieved the full-text PDF of Janse (2004), "Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech," Speech Communication 42, 155-173 (via the Max Planck pure.mpg.de repository, the cited paper at PII S0167639303001092). Experiment 2 results: Table 4 lists mean raw detection times of Linear time-compression = 572 ms (SE 9, 3% miss), Copy-fast-speech-timing = 600 ms (SE 13, 3% miss), Natural fast speech = 624 ms (SE 14, 3% miss). The text states verbatim: "detection times are fastest in the linear compression condition (572 ms), followed by the copy-fast-speech-timing (600 ms), and the natural fast speech condition (624 ms)" and "Only the linear time-compression condition and the natural fast condition differed significantly from each other ... Only the 52 ms advantage of linear compression over natural fast speech is significant." Every numeric value, the ordering, the "intermediate 600 ms," and the "significant 52 ms advantage" in the claim match the source exactly (624-572=52). The Condition effect was significant (ANOVA on inverse RT: F1(2,28)=7.4, p=0.002; F2(2,81)=4.3, p=0.039; Scheffe post-hoc significant only for linear-vs-natural, p=0.02 subject analysis). Minor technical note (not a refutation): significance was computed on inverse-RT (1/RT) data, but the paper itself attaches "significant" to the 52 ms raw-mean advantage in its own summary sentence, so the claim faithfully reproduces the authors' wording rather than overreaching. Independent corroboration (ResearchGate, Semantic Scholar, MPI listing, and later Janse 2016 work) confirms the directional finding that linear compression yields a processing advantage over natural fast speech. Source is peer-reviewed Elsevier journal = appropriate strength for the claim.

content.text: Verification complete. The claim is fully supported by the cited primary source (Janse 2004, Speech Communication 42, 155-173). I retrieved the actual PDF and confirmed every numeric value (572/600/624 ms) and the "significant 52 ms advantage" wording matches verbatim. refuted=false, high confidence.
